838: 	#pragma omp master


839: 		printf(" Level%2d in %8s: norms =%21.14e%21.14e\n", kk, title, rnm2, rnmu);


840: }


[...]


916: static void rprj3(void* pointer_r, int m1k, int m2k, int m3k, void* pointer_s, int m1j, int m2j, int m3j, int k){


[...]


923: 	double (*r)[m2k][m1k] = (double (*)[m2k][m1k])pointer_r;


924: 	double (*s)[m2j][m1j] = (double (*)[m2j][m1j])pointer_s;		


[...]


931: 	if(timeron){


932: 		#pragma omp master


933: 			timer_start(T_RPRJ3);


934: 	}


935: 	if(m1k == 3){


936: 		d1 = 2;


937: 	}else{


938: 		d1 = 1;


939: 	}


940: 	if(m2k == 3){


941: 		d2 = 2;


942: 	}else{


943: 		d2 = 1;


944: 	}


945: 	if(m3k == 3){


946: 		d3 = 2;


947: 	}else{


948: 		d3 = 1;


949: 	}


950: 


951: 	#pragma omp for


952: 	for(j3 = 1; j3 < m3j-1; j3++){


953: 		i3 = 2*j3-d3;		


954: 		for(j2 = 1; j2 < m2j-1; j2++){


955: 			i2 = 2*j2-d2;


956: 			for(j1 = 1; j1 < m1j; j1++){


957: 				i1 = 2*j1-d1;				


958: 				x1[i1] = r[i3+1][i2][i1] + r[i3+1][i2+2][i1]


959: 					+ r[i3][i2+1][i1] + r[i3+2][i2+1][i1];


960: 				y1[i1] = r[i3][i2][i1] + r[i3+2][i2][i1]


961: 					+ r[i3][i2+2][i1] + r[i3+2][i2+2][i1];


962: 			}


963: 			for(j1 = 1; j1 < m1j-1; j1++){


964: 				i1 = 2*j1-d1;				


965: 				y2 = r[i3][i2][i1+1] + r[i3+2][i2][i1+1]


966: 					+ r[i3][i2+2][i1+1] + r[i3+2][i2+2][i1+1];


967: 				x2 = r[i3+1][i2][i1+1] + r[i3+1][i2+2][i1+1]


968: 					+ r[i3][i2+1][i1+1] + r[i3+2][i2+1][i1+1];


969: 				s[j3][j2][j1] =


970: 					0.5 * r[i3+1][i2+1][i1+1]


971: 					+ 0.25 * ( r[i3+1][i2+1][i1] + r[i3+1][i2+1][i1+2] + x2)


972: 					+ 0.125 * ( x1[i1] + x1[i1+2] + y2)


973: 					+ 0.0625 * ( y1[i1] + y1[i1+2] );


974: 			}


975: 		}


976: 	}


977: 	if(timeron){


978: 		#pragma omp master


979: 			timer_stop(T_RPRJ3);


980: 	}


981: 


982: 	j=k-1;


983: 	comm3(s,m1j,m2j,m3j,j);


984: 


985: 	if(debug_vec[0] >= 1){


986: 		#pragma omp single


987: 			rep_nrm(s,m1j,m2j,m3j,(char*)"   rprj3",k-1);	


988: 	}


989: 


990: 	if(debug_vec[4] >= k){


991: 		#pragma omp single


992: 			showall(s,m1j,m2j,m3j);


993: 	}


994: }


[...]


1061: 	m1 = min(n1,18);


1062: 	m2 = min(n2,14);


1063: 	m3 = min(n3,18);


1064: 


1065: 	printf("\n");


1066: 	for(i3 = 0; i3 < m3; i3++){


1067: 		for(i2 = 0; i2 < m2; i2++){


1068: 			for(i1 = 0; i1 < m1; i1++){			


1069: 				printf("%6.3f", z[i3][i2][i1]);

1071: 			printf("\n");

1074: 	}


1075: 	printf("\n");


1076: }

The function is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:837-840,916,923-924,931-994,1061-1076.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 7.17 cycles (6.35x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 33.85 cycles (1.34x speedup).

Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • GOMP_barrier@plt: 3 occurrences►

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • IDIV: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:948

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This function is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function


The binary function does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary function is loading 432 bytes.
The binary function is storing 240 bytes.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 45.50 cycles. At this rate:

  • 9% of peak load performance is reached (9.49 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 8% of peak store performance is reached (5.27 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 36.00 cycles (1.26x speedup).

The function is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:837-840,916,923-924,931-994,1061-1076.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 7.17 cycles (6.35x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 33.85 cycles (1.34x speedup).

Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • GOMP_barrier@plt: 3 occurrences►

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • IDIV: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:948

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This function is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

The binary function does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary function is loading 432 bytes.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 45.50 cycles. At this rate:

  • 9% of peak load performance is reached (9.49 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 8% of peak store performance is reached (5.27 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 45.50 to 36.00 cycles (1.26x speedup).
